input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a 1/10‑scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology. Its main hardware layers are a lower and an upper chassis plus autonomy elements (sensors, actuators, and an ECU platform) that execute Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC). The vehicle operates in a laboratory test‑bed where it streams vehicle state, projected driving path and nearby object tracks, providing observable data that makes it suitable for digital twinning.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides three services: (1) Data Monitoring – a UI shows real‑time vehicle state, projected trajectory, object tracks, and supports data logging, saving and report generation for developers and operators; (2) Computation Offloading – the ACC algorithm (lead‑vehicle identification, distance‑based speed set‑point computation) runs on the DT (cloud) instead of the on‑board ECU, with the desired speed sent back to the vehicle for actuation; (3) Remote Management – operators can enable/disable ACC, set a target speed and issue an emergency brake command remotely via the UI, requiring low‑latency bi‑directional communication. These capabilities address monitoring, control and optimisation for both developers (troubleshooting) and operators (runtime management).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation follows ISO 23247‑1:2021 (Digital Twin framework for manufacturing) using its entity‑based reference architecture. It includes a Data Collection and Device Control Entity that gathers sensor data and issues commands, a Digital Twin Entity that hosts the ACC computation and simulation, and a User Entity that provides the UI. Functional Entities handle tasks such as data transformation, actuation and user interaction. The prototype is open‑source, hosted at McSCert (GitHub repository McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin). Communication relies on low‑latency links (protocol not specified) and the architecture can employ OPC Unified Architecture for device integration as suggested by the standard.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The DTS requires a shared notion of time across all devices to enable computation offloading, with clock synchronization accuracy bounded to ≤100 ms as dictated by safety‑critical automotive standards. This synchronization supports the ACC data‑monitoring, offloading, and remote‑management services, which also demand low end‑to‑end latency communication between the vehicle and the DT, though precise latency numbers beyond the 100 ms sync bound are not specified.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The architecture follows the ISO 23247 reference model, deploying each major entity—Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity—on separate hardware components. Within each entity, functional entities (FEs) such as the User Interface FE, Data Storage, and Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking are instantiated as ROS‑2 packages, resulting in a distributed, hierarchical deployment where a single physical twin is represented by multiple coordinated software components across distinct compute nodes.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The DTS is orchestrated as an entity‑based system per ISO 23247, with functional entities implemented as ROS 2 packages (each containing one or more ROS 2 nodes). The Physical Twin Entity hosts resource‑specific functions (sensors, actuators, compute), the Digital Twin Entity provides the digital representation and processing (including offloaded ACC computations), and the User Entity offers a 3D visualization and control‑panel UI. Communication between vehicle and DT is designed for low latency, and the overall pattern combines distributed ROS 2 middleware with the ISO‑defined entity hierarchy to manage data collection, processing, and actuation.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems is envisioned through standard interfaces defined by ISO 23247, such as OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA), which can be employed for data exchange and device control. The DTS’s UI and remote‑management capabilities imply networked connectivity that can interoperate with enterprise platforms, although the specific protocols used in the demonstrated system are not detailed in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system implements several data‑oriented functional entities. Raw sensor data from a LiDAR generates point clouds that are consumed by the 'Point Cloud LiDAR Clustering & Tracking' functional entity, which performs geometric processing (clustering and tracking) to produce object tracks. Vehicle state information (e.g., speed, position) is collected by the 'Data Collection' FE. A dedicated 'Data Translation' FE serialises all data streams, aligns message types and performs unit conversions, acting as the central data‑management component. The Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) computational model resides in the Digital Twin Entity; it is a behavioral model that determines the lead vehicle, computes a desired speed based on inter‑vehicle distance, and outputs the desired speed to the actuation side. All software components are packaged as ROS2 packages; inter‑component communication occurs over a ROS2 DDS network, while inter‑entity communication uses Wi‑Fi UDP. Model relationships are established through ROS2 topics where the output of the clustering & tracking FE feeds the ACC model, and the ACC model output feeds the actuation FE.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Physical actuation is provided by two dedicated functional entities: the 'Motor Actuation' FE, which controls vehicle propulsion, and the 'Steering Servo Actuation' FE, which controls the steering mechanism. Actuation commands originate from the ACC 'Controlling' FE in the Digital Twin Entity: the computed desired speed is sent back to the vehicle where the Motor Actuation FE executes the speed command, and remote management commands (e.g., emergency braking) are also routed to the Motor Actuation FE. Actuation interfaces are exposed to the DT over the Wi‑Fi UDP link and are realized as ROS2 services/topics. Safety constraints are implied by the requirement for low‑latency communication (≤100 ms clock synchronization) to ensure timely execution of braking or speed adjustments.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing infrastructure consists of a LiDAR sensor that provides raw point‑cloud data and on‑board vehicle‑state sensors (e.g., speed, position) that publish vehicle‑state messages. The LiDAR data is processed by the 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking' FE after being pre‑processed by the 'Data Pre‑Processing' FE. Sensor data is published on ROS2 topics within the Physical Twin Entity and is transmitted to the Digital Twin Entity over a Wi‑Fi network using UDP at a rate sufficient for real‑time visualization (exact sampling frequency not specified). Data collection is handled by the 'Data Collection' FE, which aggregates sensor streams for storage and reporting.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Physical sensor data (LiDAR point‑cloud, motor status, steering servo position, and overall vehicle state) are captured by the Physical Twin Entity. The raw LiDAR point‑cloud is first processed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking software component (Data Pre‑Processing FE) which converts the raw 2‑D point list into clustered tracks. All data streams are then handed to the Data Translation FE, which serializes the information, aligns message types and performs required unit conversions. Communication between software components inside an entity uses a ROS 2 network, while data that crosses entity boundaries (e.g., from the Physical Twin Entity to the Digital Twin Entity) is transmitted over a Wi‑Fi link using UDP at the transport layer. The processed vehicle‑state information, object tracks and projected driving path are subsequently displayed in real‑time on the 3D Visualization FE of the User Entity.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands are generated by the ACC service executing in the Digital Twin Entity. After computing the desired speed based on the lead‑vehicle distance, the ACC service sends a numeric desired‑speed command, as well as Boolean enable/disable ACC commands and an emergency‑brake signal, back to the Physical Twin Entity over the same Wi‑Fi UDP channel. The incoming commands are received by the Actuation FE, which forwards them to the vehicle actuators (motor, steering servo). The vehicle returns updated actuator status and vehicle‑state information, allowing the system to confirm command execution. Validation, authentication and conflict‑resolution mechanisms are not detailed in the documents, but the architecture requires low end‑to‑end latency to satisfy safety‑critical constraints.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DTS uses a two‑layer communication stack. Inside each entity, software components are linked through a ROS 2 network (publish‑subscribe over DDS). Across entities, messages travel over a Wi‑Fi network employing UDP at the transport layer. The system must meet a synchronization accuracy of 100 ms, which defines the maximum tolerated latency for safety‑critical exchanges. No specific bandwidth figures are given. Redundancy or security protocols (e.g., TLS, encryption) are not mentioned in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Each functional element (FE) is implemented as an independent ROS 2 package that can be deployed on distinct hardware resources. Computation‑intensive services such as the ACC algorithm and Data Translation are off‑loaded to the Digital Twin Entity, which may run on cloud or edge resources, while sensor pre‑processing and actuation reside on the Physical Twin hardware. The architecture therefore supports a hybrid cloud‑edge deployment model, but the documents do not specify exact computational or storage sizing, scalability mechanisms, or availability guarantees.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture. During the design phase the team enumerates key software components (see Table I) and their relationships to the physical sensors and actuators. Each component is then mapped to a Functional Entity (FE) and realized as an individual ROS 2 package; a package may contain one or more ROS 2 nodes that implement the FE’s functionality. The resulting entities – Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity and User Entity – are deployed on separate hardware units (e.g., the vehicle’s compute platform for the Physical Twin, a server for the Digital Twin, and a workstation for the User Interface). Development was performed by two graduate students as a case study, and the implementation uses open‑source tools (ROS 2, OPC UA as an optional protocol) but the documents do not specify explicit version‑control or testing practices.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system is built around compliance with ISO 23247 (parts 1‑4), using its entity‑based reference architecture (Observable Manufacturing Element, Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity). Functional Entities are instantiated as ROS 2 packages, which can interoperate with industry protocols such as OPC Unified Architecture. For safety‑critical automotive contexts the authors argue that ISO 26262 should be added as a first‑class standard, and they reference AUTOSAR as the de‑facto automotive software architecture that inspires the hardware‑agnostic deployment of FEs. Timing requirements of ≤ 100 ms clock synchronisation for safety‑critical vehicular applications are mentioned, reflecting alignment with automotive timing standards.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture


1 [https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin](https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin)
2 [https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU](https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU)

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.

We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design

Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement

each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control

the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity

automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_

standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.

V. R EFLECTION AND C ONCLUSIONS


We reported on a case study of an automotive DT, through
which we investigate the fitness of the ISO 23247 standard
in engineering such systems. Our experiences highlight both
benefits and limitations of the standard in automotive settings.
On a positive note, we found the standard’s reference
architecture (RA) to be **a useful starting point for auto-**
**motive DTs** as it provides guidance for organizing blocks
of functionality. Additionally, we found the standard **easy to**
**use** as it does not assume substantial DT expertise and has a
comfortable learning curve. Our case was developed by two
graduate students, starting from a course project. [4] Through
this case study, we also highlight the **convenient generality**
of ISO 23247. The independence from specific format and
protocol requirements, as well as the ability to rely on domainspecific technologies in a straightforward manner, facilitated
the transfer of the RA to a new domain (automotive).
On a critical note, rigid adherence to the RA may lead to an
**under-utilization of resources** . In contrast to OMEs, vehicles
are resource-rich systems of their own. Thus, we recommend
promoting the physical twin to a first-class entity in automotive
DT architectures. This will allow for deploying FEs on the
vehicle, potentially in a flexible and dynamic fashion. For
example, our case study deploys the Data Pre-Processing FE
and the Actuation FE onto the physical twin, as opposed to
the DT Entity as prescribed by the ISO 23247 RA. Flexibility
in the deployment of FEs is at the heart of existing automotive
standards—e.g., AUTOSAR, the _de facto_ automotive software
architecture standard—where SW components are defined in a
hardware-agnostic manner, making it easier to retrofit existing
automotive architectures into automotive DT architectures.

Our case study further suggests that the RA is **insufficient for**
**safety-critical applications such as automotive** . Therefore,
we recommend safety to be promoted to a first-class citizen
in automotive DT standards by relying on existing automotive
safety standards—e.g., ISO 26262 [38].
Our work is a first look into the benefits and challenges of
adopting a standardized DT architecture for automotive DTs.
In order to aid the development of proper domain-specific
standards, we call for additional research into the topic, with
a particular emphasis on safety, security, data management,
and considering the regulated nature of the automotive domain, compatibility with existing standards. Additionally, we
encourage the development of automotive DT case studies and
exemplars to provide empirical evidence for further research.
In future work, we plan to research the safety aspects of
automotive DTs in more detail and elicit recommendations

**under-utilization of resources** . In contrast to OMEs, vehicles
are resource-rich systems of their own. Thus, we recommend
promoting the physical twin to a first-class entity in automotive
DT architectures. This will allow for deploying FEs on the
vehicle, potentially in a flexible and dynamic fashion. For
example, our case study deploys the Data Pre-Processing FE
and the Actuation FE onto the physical twin, as opposed to
the DT Entity as prescribed by the ISO 23247 RA. Flexibility
in the deployment of FEs is at the heart of existing automotive
standards—e.g., AUTOSAR, the _de facto_ automotive software
architecture standard—where SW components are defined in a
hardware-agnostic manner, making it easier to retrofit existing
automotive architectures into automotive DT architectures.

Our case study further suggests that the RA is **insufficient for**
**safety-critical applications such as automotive** . Therefore,
we recommend safety to be promoted to a first-class citizen
in automotive DT standards by relying on existing automotive
safety standards—e.g., ISO 26262 [38].
Our work is a first look into the benefits and challenges of
adopting a standardized DT architecture for automotive DTs.
In order to aid the development of proper domain-specific
standards, we call for additional research into the topic, with
a particular emphasis on safety, security, data management,
and considering the regulated nature of the automotive domain, compatibility with existing standards. Additionally, we
encourage the development of automotive DT case studies and
exemplars to provide empirical evidence for further research.
In future work, we plan to research the safety aspects of
automotive DTs in more detail and elicit recommendations
